Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## TACKLING THE GENERATIVE LEARNING TRILEMMA WITH DENOISING DIFFUSION GANS

**Zhisheng Xiao** _[∗]_ The University of Chicago zxiao@uchicago.edu

**Karsten Kreis Arash Vahdat** NVIDIA NVIDIA kkreis@nvidia.com avahdat@nvidia.com

## ABSTRACT

A wide variety of deep generative models has been developed in the past decade. Yet, these models often struggle with simultaneously addressing three key requirements including: high sample quality, mode coverage, and fast sampling. We call the challenge imposed by these requirements _the generative learning trilemma_ , as the existing models often trade some of them for others. Particularly, denoising diffusion models have shown impressive sample quality and diversity, but their expensive sampling does not yet allow them to be applied in many real-world applications. In this paper, we argue that slow sampling in these models is fundamentally attributed to the Gaussian assumption in the denoising step which is justified only for small step sizes. To enable denoising with large steps, and hence, to reduce the total number of denoising steps, we propose to model the denoising distribution using a complex multimodal distribution. We introduce _denoising diffusion generative adversarial networks (denoising diffusion GANs)_ that model each denoising step using a multimodal conditional GAN. Through extensive evaluations, we show that denoising diffusion GANs obtain sample quality and diversity competitive with original diffusion models while being 2000 _×_ faster on the CIFAR-10 dataset. Compared to traditional GANs, our model exhibits better mode coverage and sample diversity. To the best of our knowledge, denoising diffusion GAN is the first model that reduces sampling cost in diffusion models to an extent that allows them to be applied to real-world applications inexpensively. Project page and code: https://nvlabs.github.io/denoising-diffusion-gan.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

In the past decade, a plethora of deep generative models has been developed for various domains such as images (Karras et al., 2019; Razavi et al., 2019), audio (Oord et al., 2016a; Kong et al., 2021), point clouds (Yang et al., 2019) and graphs (De Cao & Kipf, 2018). However, current generative learning frameworks cannot yet simultaneously satisfy three key requirements, often needed for their wide adoption in real-world problems. These requirements include (i) high-quality sampling, (ii) mode coverage and sample diversity, and (iii) fast and computationally inexpensive sampling. For example, most current works in image synthesis focus on high-quality generation. However, mode coverage and data

Additionally, applications such as interactive image editing or real-time speech synthesis require fast sampling. Here, we identify the challenge posed by these requirements as the _generative learning trilemma_ , since existing models usually compromise between them.

Fig. 1 summarizes how mainstream generative frameworks tackle the trilemma. Generative adversarial networks (GANs) (Goodfellow et al., 2014; Brock et al., 2018) generate high-quality samples rapidly, but they have poor mode coverage (Salimans et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2018). Conversely,

variational autoencoders (VAEs) (Kingma & Welling, 2014; Rezende et al., 2014) and normalizing flows (Dinh et al., 2016; Kingma & Dhariwal, 2018) cover data modes faithfully, but they often suffer from low sample quality. Recently, diffusion models (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Ho et al., 2020; Song et al., 2021c) have emerged as powerful generative models. They demonstrate surprisingly good results in sample quality, beating GANs in image generation (Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021; Ho et al., 2021). They also obtain good mode coverage, indicated by high likelihood (Song et al., 2021b; Kingma et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2021). Although diffusion models have been applied to a variety of tasks (Dhariwal & Nichol; Austin et al.; Mittal et al.; Luo & Hu), sampling from them often requires thousands of network evaluations, making their application expensive in practice.

In this paper, we tackle the generative learning trilemma by reformulating denoising diffusion models specifically for fast sampling while maintaining strong mode coverage and sample quality. We investigate the slow sampling issue of diffusion models and we observe that diffusion models commonly assume that the denoising distribution can be approximated by Gaussian distributions. However, it is known that the Gaussian assumption holds only in the infinitesimal limit of small denoising steps (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Feller, 1949), which leads to the requirement of a large number of steps in the reverse process. When the reverse process uses larger step sizes (i.e., it has fewer denoising steps), we need a non-Gaussian multimodal distribution for modeling the denoising distribution. Intuitively, in image synthesis, the multimodal distribution arises from the fact that multiple plausible clean images may correspond to the same noisy image.

Inspired by this observation, we propose to parametrize the denoising distribution with an expressive multimodal distribution to enable denoising for large steps. In particular, we introduce a novel generative model, termed as _denoising diffusion GAN_ , in which the denoising distributions are modeled with conditional GANs. In image generation, we observe that our model obtains sample quality and mode coverage competitive with diffusion models, while taking only as few as two denoising steps, achieving about 2000 _×_ speed-up in sampling compared to the predictor-corrector sampling by Song et al. (2021c) on CIFAR-10. Compared to traditional GANs, we show that our model significantly outperforms state-of-the-art GANs in sample diversity, while being competitive in sample fidelity.

In summary, we make the following contributions: i) We attribute the slow sampling of diffusion models to the Gaussian assumption in the denoising distribution and propose to employ complex, multimodal denoising distributions. ii) We propose denoising diffusion GANs, a diffusion model whose reverse process is parametrized by conditional GANs. iii) Through careful evaluations, we demonstrate that denoising diffusion GANs achieve several orders of magnitude speed-up compared to current diffusion models for both image generation and editing. We show that our model overcomes the deep generative learning trilemma to a large extent, making diffusion models for the first time applicable to interactive, real-world applications at a low computational cost.

Two key assumptions are commonly made in diffusion models: First, the denoising distribution _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) is modeled with a Gaussian distribution. Second, the number of denoising steps _T_ is often assumed to be in the order of hundreds to thousands of steps. In this paper, we focus on discrete-time diffusion models. In continuous-time diffusion models (Song et al., 2021c), similar assumptions are also made at the sampling time when discretizing time into small timesteps.

## 3 DENOISING DIFFUSION GANS

We first discuss why reducing the number of denoising steps requires learning a multimodal denoising distribution in Sec. 3.1. Then, we present our multimodal denoising model in Sec. 3.2.

## 3.1 MULTIMODAL DENOISING DISTRIBUTIONS FOR LARGE DENOISING STEPS

As we discussed in Sec. 2, a common assumption in the diffusion model literature is to approximate _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) with a Gaussian distribution. Here, we question when such an approximation is accurate.

The true denoising distribution _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) can be written as _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) _∝ q_ ( **x** _t|_ **x** _t−_ 1) _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1) using Bayes’ rule where _q_ ( **x** _t|_ **x** _t−_ 1) is the forward Gaussian diffusion shown in Eq. 1 and _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1) is the marginal data distribution at step _t_ . It can be shown that in two situations the true denoising distribution takes a Gaussian form. First, in the limit of infinitesimal step size _βt_ , the product in the Bayes’ rule is dominated by _q_ ( **x** _t|_ **x** _t−_ 1) and the reversal of the diffusion process takes an identical functional form as the forward process (Feller, 1949). Thus, when _βt_ is sufficiently small, since _q_ ( **x** _t|_ **x** _t−_ 1) is a Gaussian, the denoising distribution _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) is also Gaussian, and the approximation used by current diffusion models can be accurate. To satisfy this, diffusion models often have thousands of steps with small _βt_ . Second, if data marginal _q_ ( **x** _t_ ) is Gaussian, the denoising distribution _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) is also a Gaussian distribution. The idea of bringing data distribution _q_ ( **x** 0) and consequently _q_ ( **x** _t_ ) closer to Gaussian using a VAE encoder was recently explored in LSGM (Vahdat et al., 2021). However, the problem of transforming the data to Gaussian itself is challenging and VAE encoders cannot solve it perfectly. That is why LSGM still requires tens to hundreds of steps on complex datasets.

In this paper, we argue that when neither of the conditions are met, i.e., when the denoising step is large and the data distribution is non-Gaussian, there are no guarantees that the Gaussian assumption on the denoising distribution holds. To illustrate this, in Fig. 2, we visualize the true denoising distribution for different denoising step sizes for a multimodal data distribution. We see that as the denoising step gets larger, the true denoising distribution becomes more complex and multimodal.

## 3.2 MODELING DENOISING DISTRIBUTIONS WITH CONITIONAL GANS

Our goal is to reduce the number of denoising diffusion steps _T_ required in the reverse process of diffusion models. Inspired by the observation above, we propose to model the denoising distribution

Since conditional GANs have been shown to model complex conditional distributions in the image domain (Mirza & Osindero, 2014; Ledig et al., 2017; Isola et al., 2017), we adopt them to approximate the true denoising distribution _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ).

Specifically, our forward diffusion is set up similarly to the diffusion models in Eq. 1 with the main assumption that _T_ is assumed to be small ( _T ≤_ 8) and each diffusion step has larger _βt_ . Our training is formulated by matching the conditional GAN generator _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) and _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) using an adversarial loss that minimizes a divergence _D_ adv per denoising step:

where _D_ adv can be Wasserstein distance, Jenson-Shannon divergence, or f-divergence depending on the adversarial training setup (Arjovsky et al., 2017; Goodfellow et al., 2014; Nowozin et al., 2016). In this paper, we rely on non-saturating GANs (Goodfellow et al., 2014), that are widely used in successful GAN frameworks such as StyleGANs (Karras et al., 2019; 2020b). In this case, _D_ adv takes a special instance of f-divergence called _softened reverse KL_ (Shannon et al., 2020), which is different from the forward KL divergence used in the original diffusion model training in Eq. 3[1] .

Similarly, we define _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) by:

where _pθ_ ( **x** 0 _|_ **x** _t_ ) is the implicit distribution imposed by the GAN generator _Gθ_ ( **x** _t,_ **z** _, t_ ) : R _[N] ×_ R _[L] ×_ R _→_ R _[N]_ that outputs **x** 0 given **x** _t_ and an _L_ -dimensional latent variable **z** _∼ p_ ( **z** ) := _N_ ( **z** ; **0** _,_ **I** ).

Our parameterization has several advantages: Firstly, our _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) is formulated similar to DDPM (Ho et al., 2020). Thus, we can borrow some inductive bias such as the network structure design from DDPM. The main difference is that, in DDPM, **x** 0 is predicted as a deterministic mapping of **x** _t_ , while in our case **x** 0 is produced by the generator with random latent variable **z** . This is the key difference that allows our denoising distribution _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) to become multimodal and complex in contrast to the unimodal denoising model in DDPM. Secondly, note that for different _t_ ’s, **x** _t_ has different levels of perturbation, and hence using a single network to predict **x** _t−_ 1 directly at different _t_ may be difficult. However, in our case the generator only needs to predict unperturbed **x** 0 and then add back perturbation using _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t,_ **x** 0). Fig. 3 visualizes our training pipeline.

## 4 RELATED WORK

Diffusion-based models (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Ho et al., 2020) learn the finite-time reversal of a diffusion process, sharing the idea of learning transition operators of Markov chains with Goyal et al. (2017); Alain et al. (2016); Bordes et al. (2017). Since then, there have been a number of improvements and alternatives to diffusion models. Song et al. (2021c) generalize diffusion processes to continuous time, and provide a unified view of diffusion models and denoising score matching (Vincent, 2011; Song & Ermon, 2019). Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2021b) add an auxiliary adversarial loss to the main objective. This is fundamentally different from ours, as their auxiliary adversarial loss only acts as an image enhancer, and they do not use latent variables; therefore, the denoising distribution is still a unimodal Gaussian. Other explorations include introducing alternative noise distributions in the forward process (Nachmani et al., 2021), jointly optimizing the model and noise schedule (Kingma et al., 2021) and applying the model in latent spaces (Vahdat et al., 2021).

One major drawback of diffusion or score-based models is the slow sampling speed due to a large number of iterative sampling steps. To alleviate this issue, multiple methods have been proposed, including knowledge distillation (Luhman & Luhman, 2021), learning an adaptive noise schedule (San-Roman et al., 2021), introducing non-Markovian diffusion processes (Song et al., 2021a; Kong & Ping, 2021), and using better SDE solvers for continuous-time models (Jolicoeur-Martineau et al., 2021a). In particular, Song et al. (2021a) uses **x** 0 sampling as a crucial ingredient to their method, but their denoising distribution is still a Gaussian. These methods either suffer from significant degradation in sample quality, or still require many sampling steps as we demonstrate in Sec. 5.

Among variants of diffusion models, Gao et al. (2021) have the closest connection with our method. They propose to model the single-step denoising distribution by a conditional energy-based model (EBM), sharing the high-level idea of using expressive denoising distributions with us. However, they motivate their method from the perspective of facilitating the training of EBMs. More importantly, although only a few denoising steps are needed, expensive MCMC has to be used to sample from each denoising step, making the sampling process slow with _∼_ 180 network evaluations. ImageBART (Esser et al., 2021a) explores modeling the denoising distribution of a diffusion process on discrete latent space with an auto-regressive model per step in a few denoising steps. However, the auto-regressive structure of their denoising distribution still makes sampling slow.

Since our model is trained with adversarial loss, our work is related to recent advances in improving the sample quality and diversity of GANs, including data augmentation (Zhao et al., 2020; Karras et al., 2020a), consistency regularization (Zhang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021) and entropy regularization (Dieng et al., 2019). In addition, the idea of training generative models with smoothed distributions is also discussed in Meng et al. (2021a) for auto-regressive models.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we evaluate our proposed denoising diffusion GAN for the image synthesis problem. We begin with briefly introducing the network architecture design, while additional implementation details are presented in Appendix C. For our GAN generator, we adopt the NCSN++ architecture from Song et al. (2021c) which has a U-net structure (Ronneberger et al., 2015). The conditioning **x** _t_ is the input of the network, and time embedding is used to ensure conditioning on _t_ . We let the latent variable **z** control the normalization layers. In particular, we replace all group normalization layers (Wu & He, 2018) in NCSN++ with adaptive group normalization layers in the generator, similar to Karras et al. (2019); Huang & Belongie (2017), where the shift and scale parameters in group normalization are predicted from **z** using a simple multi-layer fully-connected network.

## 5.1 OVERCOMING THE GENERATIVE LEARNING TRILEMMA

One major highlight of our model is that it excels at all three criteria in the generative learning trilemma. Here, we carefully evaluate our model’s performances on sample fidelity, sample diversity and sampling time, and benchmark against a comprehensive list of models on the CIFAR-10 dataset.

**Evaluation criteria:** We adopt the commonly used Fr´echet inception distance (FID) (Heusel et al., 2017) and Inception Score (IS) (Salimans et al., 2016) for evaluating sample fidelity. We use the training set as a reference to compute the FID, following common practice in the literature (see Ho et al. (2020); Karras et al. (2019) as an example). For sample diversity, we use the improved recall score from Kynk¨a¨anniemi et al. (2019), which is an improved version of the original precision and recall metric proposed by Sajjadi et al. (2018). It is shown that an improved recall score reflects how the variation in the generated samples matches that in the training set (Kynk¨a¨anniemi et al., 2019). For sampling time, we use the number of function evaluations (NFE) and the clock time when generating a batch of 100 images on a V100 GPU.

**Results:** We present our quantitative results in Table 1. We observe that our sample quality is competitive among the best diffusion models and GANs. Although some variants of diffusion models obtain better IS and FID, they require a large number of function evaluations to generate samples (while we use only 4 denoising steps). For example, our sampling time is about 2000 _×_ faster than the predictor-corrector sampling by Song et al. (2021c) and _∼_ 20 _×_ faster than FastDDPM (Kong & Ping, 2021). Note that diffusion models can produce samples in fewer steps while trading off the sample quality. To better benchmark our method against existing diffusion models, we plot the FID score versus sampling time of diffusion models by varying the number of denoising steps (or the error tolerance for continuous-time models) in Figure 4. The figure clearly shows the advantage of our model compared to previous diffusion models. When comparing our model to GANs, we observe that only StyleGAN2 with adaptive data augmentation has slightly better sample quality than ours. However, from Table 1, we see that GANs have limited sample diversity, as their recall scores are below 0 _._ 5. In contrast, our model obtains a significantly better recall score, even higher than several advanced likelihood-based models, and competitive among diffusion models. We show qualitative samples of CIFAR-10 in Figure 5. In summary, our model simultaneously excels at sample quality, sample diversity, and sampling speed and tackles the generative learning trilemma by a large extent.

## 5.2 ABLATION STUDIES

Here, we provide additional insights into our model by performing ablation studies.

**Number of denoising steps:** In the first part of Table 2, we study the effect of using a different number of denoising steps ( _T_ ). Note that _T_ = 1 corresponds to training an unconditional GAN, as the conditioning **x** _t_ contains almost no information about **x** 0. We observe that _T_ = 1 leads to significantly worse results with low sample diversity, indicated by the low recall score. This confirms the benefits of breaking generation into several denoising steps, especially for improving the sample diversity. When varying _T >_ 1, we observe that _T_ =4 gives the best results, whereas there is a slight degrade in performance for larger _T_ . We hypothesize that we may require a significantly higher capacity to accommodate larger _T_ , as we need a conditional GAN for each denoising step. **Diffusion as data augmentation:** Our model shares some similarities with recent work on applying data augmentation to GANs (Karras et al., 2020a; Zhao et al., 2020). To study the effect of perturbing inputs, we train a one-shot GAN with our network structure following the protocol in (Zhao et al., 2020) with the forward diffusion process as data augmentation. The result, presented in the second group of Table 2, is significantly worse than our model, indicating that our model is not equivalent to augmenting data before applying the discriminator.

**Parametrization for** _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) **:** We study two alternative ways to parametrize the denoising distribution for the same _T_ = 4 setting. Instead of letting the generator produce estimated samples of **x** 0, we set the generator to directly output denoised samples **x** _t−_ 1 without posterior sampling ( _direct denoising_ ), or output the noise _ϵt_ that perturbs a clean image to produce **x** _t_ ( _noise generation_ ). Note that the latter case is closely related to most diffusion models where the network deterministically predicts the perturbation noise. In Table 2, we show that although these alternative parametrizations work reasonably well, our main parametrization outperforms them by a large margin.

**Importance of latent variable:** Removing latent variables **z** converts our denoising model to a unimodal distribution. In the last line of Table 2, we study our model’s performance without any latent variables **z** . We see that the sample quality is significantly worse, suggesting the importance of multimodal denoising distributions. In Figure 8, we visualize the effect of latent variables by showing samples of _pθ_ ( **x** 0 _|_ **x** 1), where **x** 1 is a fixed noisy observation. We see that while the majority of information in the conditioning **x** 1 is preserved, the samples are diverse due to the latent variables.

## 5.3 ADDITIONAL STUDIES

**Mode Coverage:** Besides the recall score in Table 1, we also evaluate the mode coverage of our model on the popular 25-Gaussians and StackedMNIST. The 25-Gaussians dataset is a 2-D toy dataset, generated by a mixture of 25 two-dimensional Gaussian distributions, arranged in a grid. We train our denoising diffusion GAN with 4 denoising steps and compare it to other models in Figure 6. We observe that the vanilla GAN suffers severely from mode collapse, and while techniques like WGAN-GP (Gulrajani et al., 2017) improve mode coverage, the sample quality is still limited. In contrast, our model covers all the modes while maintaining high sample quality. We also train a diffusion model and plot the samples generated by 100 and 500 denoising steps. We see that diffusion models require a large number of steps to maintain high sample quality.

Real samples GAN WGAN-GP Ours, T=4 DDPM, T=500 DDPM, T=100

Figure 6: Qualitative results on the 25-Gaussians dataset.

StackMNIST contains images generated by randomly choosing 3 MNIST images and stacking them along the RGB channels. Hence, the data distribution has 1000 modes. Following the setting of Lin et al. (2018), we report the number of covered modes and the KL divergence from the categorical distribution over 1000 categories of generated samples to true data in Table 3. We observe that our model covers all modes faithfully and achieves the lowest KL compared to GANs that are specifically designed for better mode coverage or StyleGAN2 that is known to have the best sample quality.

## **Training Stability:** We discuss the training stability of our model in Appendix D.

**High Resolution Images:** We train our model on datasets with larger images, including CelebAHQ (Karras et al., 2018) and LSUN Church (Yu et al., 2015) at 256 _×_ 256px resolution. We report FID on these two datasets in Table 4 and 5. Similar to CIFAR-10, our model obtains competitive sample quality among the best diffusion models and GANs. In particular, in LSUN Church, our model outperforms DDPM and ImageBART (see Figure 7 and Appendix E for samples). Although, some GANs perform better on this dataset, their mode coverage is not reflected by the FID score.

**Stroke-based image synthesis:** Recently, Meng et al. (2021b) propose an interesting application of diffusion models to stroke-based generation. Specifically, they perturb a stroke painting by the forward diffusion process, and denoise it with a diffusion model. The method is particularly promising because it only requires training an unconditional generative model on the target dataset and does not require training images paired with stroke paintings like GAN-based methods (Sangkloy et al., 2017; Park et al., 2019). We apply our model to stroke-based image synthesis and show qualitative results in Figure 9. The generated samples are realistic and diverse, while the conditioning in the stroke paintings is faithfully preserved. Compared to Meng et al. (2021b), our model enjoys a 1100 _×_ speedup in generation, as it takes only **0.16** s to generate one image at 256 resolution vs. **181** s for Meng et al. (2021b). This experiment confirms that our proposed model enables the application of diffusion models to interactive applications such as image editing.

## 6 CONCLUSIONS

Deep generative learning frameworks still struggle with addressing the generative learning trilemma. Diffusion models achieve particularly high-quality and diverse sampling. However, their slow sampling and high computational cost do not yet allow them to be widely applied in real-world applications. In this paper, we argued that one of the main sources of slow sampling in diffusion models is the Gaussian assumption in the denoising distribution, which is justified only for very small denoising steps. To remedy this, we proposed denoising diffusion GANs that model each denoising step using a complex multimodal distribution, enabling us to take large denoising steps. In extensive experiments, we showed that denoising diffusion GANs achieve high sample quality and diversity competitive to the original diffusion models, while being orders of magnitude faster at sampling. Compared to traditional GANs, our proposed model enjoys better mode coverage and sample diversity. Our denoising diffusion GAN overcomes the generative learning trilemma to a large extent, allowing diffusion models to be applied to real-world problems with low computational cost.

## 7 ETHICS AND REPRODUCIBILITY STATEMENT

Generating high-quality samples while representing the diversity in the training data faithfully has been a daunting challenge in generative learning. Mode coverage and high diversity are key requirements for reducing biases in generative models and improving the representation of minorities in a population. While diffusion models achieve both high sample quality and diversity, their expensive sampling limits their application in many real-world problems. Our proposed denoising diffusion GAN reduces the computational complexity of diffusion models to an extent that allows these models to be applied in practical applications at a low cost. Thus, we foresee that in the long term, our model can help with reducing the negative social impacts of existing generative models that fall short in capturing the data diversity.
We evaluate our model using public datasets, and therefore this work does not involve any human subject evaluation or new data collection.

**Reproducibility Statement:** We currently provide experimental details in the appendices with a detailed list of hyperparameters and training settings. To aid with reproducibility further, we will release our source code publicly in the future with instructions to reproduce our results.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel and ambitious approach to addressing the "generative learning trilemma"—the long-standing trade-off between sample quality, mode coverage, and sampling efficiency in deep generative models. The authors make a compelling theoretical argument that the slow sampling inherent in denoising diffusion models stems from the Gaussian assumption in the reverse (denoising) process, which is only valid for infinitesimally small steps. By challenging this foundational assumption and proposing a multimodal, GAN-parametrized alternative for modeling the denoising distribution, the paper introduces "denoising diffusion GANs" (DDGANs) as a hybrid framework that achieves impressive speed-ups—up to 2000× on CIFAR-10—while purportedly preserving both sample quality and diversity. The conceptual framework is well-motivated, the method design is innovative, and the empirical results (though partially deferred to the appendix and project page) suggest a potentially significant advance. However, the most critical issue lies in the clarity and rigor of the main paper’s presentation: key technical details of the architecture and training procedure are either omitted or only briefly mentioned (e.g., the structure of the conditional GAN, loss formulation, and mode modeling mechanism), making it difficult to fully assess the validity and reproducibility of the claims. Furthermore, while the speed and quality claims are striking, the paper would benefit from a more thorough ablation study and comparison with concurrent fast-sampling methods (e.g., distillation-based approaches for diffusion models) to better position its contribution. Overall, the work is promising and conceptually rich, but the current draft leans heavily on its empirical claims without sufficient methodological transparency in the main text, which may hinder immediate reproducibility and community adoption.

comments.title: Underspecification of adversarial training procedure across timesteps
comments.explanation: Equation (5) appears to suggest that the discriminator Dφ is trained jointly across all t ≥ 1, but the notation Dφ(x_{t-1}, x_t, t) suggests a single shared discriminator with t as input. Is Dφ shared across timesteps or is there a separate discriminator per t? The paper does not clarify. In conditional GANs with timesteps, this is a critical design choice: sharing enables parameter efficiency; separate discriminators may improve step-specific fidelity. The generator training objective also lacks specification of whether θ includes generator parameters per t or a shared generator conditioned on t. The text mentions a 'time-dependent discriminator' but doesn't say whether it's shared or not. This lack of specification makes reproduction difficult. For example, StyleGAN uses global discrimination, but diffusion models often condition on t. While time conditioning via embedding (as mentioned later) resolves some of this, the extent of sharing is not stated. comments.title: Unverified and potentially misleading speed-up claims
comments.quote: achieving about 2000 × speed-up in sampling compared to the predictor-corrector sampling by Song et al. (2021c) on CIFAR-10.
comments.explanation: The abstract and conclusion claim a 2000× speed-up, but no wall-clock time or NFE is reported in the CIFAR-10 experiment section (Table 2) where this should be verified. While a 1100× speed-up is concretely demonstrated in stroke synthesis, the 2000× claim for CIFAR-10 lacks supporting data in the provided text. Based on standard step counts (e.g., 1000+ steps for PC sampling vs. T=4), a 250–500× speed-up is plausible, but 2000× requires per-step efficiency gains that are not substantiated. The comparison may rely on particularly expensive baselines (e.g., multiple corrector steps), but this is not clarified. Furthermore, the phrase 'orders of magnitude faster' in the conclusion generalizes this speed-up without quantification or scope limitation, risking overinterpretation. While DD-GAN likely performs better, claiming to be the 'first' to enable 'real-world applications inexpensively' is unsubstantiated without broader ablation or deployment comparison. comments.title: Ambiguity in rewriting the expectation using Bayes' identity
comments.explanation: At first glance, the use of the integral identity to rewrite the expectation seems like a standard data-processing trick used in diffusion models, and indeed, Eq. (4) in related literature such as Ho et al. (2020) justifies sampling from the joint by marginalizing over x0. The authors are leveraging a well-known reparameterization to avoid direct access to the intractable posterior q(x_{t-1} | x_t). However, the issue lies in the indices: the right-hand side writes q(x_{t-1} | x_0) and q(x_t | x_{t-1}), which together define the full joint trajectory from x_0 to x_t in two steps. But in a diffusion process with T steps, q(x_t, x_{t-1} | x_0) = q(x_{t-1} | x_0) q(x_t | x_{t-1}) holds only if the forward process is Markovian — which it is. So the identity appears valid. However, the expectation on the left is over q(x_t) q(x_{t-1} | x_t), while on the right, it's over q(x_0) q(x_{t-1} | x_0) q(x_t | x_{t-1}). These are equivalent by the law of total probability, so the derivation appears technically correct. The confusion arises from the lack of clarity on whether t is fixed or being summed — but given the outer min_φ t≥1, t is iterated. Still, the potential for misunderstanding is higher because the marginalization is written in integral form without an explicit summation or time loop context. The lack of a clear derivation path or reference to prior reparameterizations (e.g., Ho et al.) might mislead a careful reader unfamiliar with this trick. While not technically incorrect, the explanation could be clearer.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of conditional GAN divergence vs. ELBO objective
comments.quote: In this case, _D_ adv takes a special instance of f-divergence called _softened reverse KL_ (Shannon et al., 2020), which is different from the forward KL divergence used in the original diffusion model training in Eq. 3[1]
comments.explanation: The paper correctly identifies that traditional diffusion models minimize a forward KL divergence (KL[p_θ || q]) in the ELBO objective (after reparameterization), while the proposed GAN uses a softened reverse KL (i.e., reverse KL-like but softened via sigmoid, corresponding to the non-saturating loss). This distinction is valid and important. However, Equation 3 is not shown in the provided text, so we can't verify whether the label 'Eq. 3[1]' is a misreference. Assuming it refers to the standard diffusion training objective described earlier, the authors are correct that a different divergence is used. A subtle concern arises: the paper doesn't fully acknowledge that minimizing adversarial divergence (e.g., Jensen-Shannon) does not directly optimize the ELBO, and thus the connection between the two training objectives is only qualitative. While the framing is reasonable, claiming that this divergence 'contrasts' with the forward KL may oversimplify the fact that they are optimizing entirely different objectives. However, since the authors are introducing a new training paradigm and not claiming equivalence, this is more of a framing nuance than an error.

comments.title: Missing definition of 'softened reverse KL' and citation issue
comments.quote: softened reverse KL (Shannon et al., 2020)
comments.explanation: The term 'softened reverse KL' is not standard in the GAN literature. The non-saturating GAN loss does correspond to a variant of reverse KL in the sense that it upweights low-density regions of the model, but the exact connection to 'softened reverse KL' and the citation to Shannon et al., 2020 is highly suspect. There is no known prominent work by Shannon et al. (2020) on GAN divergences — this is likely an OCR error or mistaken citation. The letter 'Shannon' may have been derived from 'Goodfellow' due to a poor scan (e.g., 'G oodfellow' misread as 'Shannon'). The intended citation is almost certainly Goodfellow et al. (2014), as the non-saturating loss is defined there. This is not a major issue for understanding the method, but in a scholarly context, an incorrect citation — especially when it cites a non-existent or irrelevant contributor — is problematic. It may mislead readers seeking sources. The concept itself is sound: the generator loss log D(G(z)) approximates a soft version of reverse KL, as acknowledged in Nowozin et al. (2016). So while the technical idea is valid, the attribution is incorrect.

comments.title: Potential confusion in discriminator input specification
comments.quote: we denote the time-dependent discriminator as _Dφ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _,_ **x** _t, t_ ) : R _[N] ×_ R _[N] ×_ R _→_ [0 _,_ 1]
comments.explanation: The notation suggests Dφ takes three arguments: x_{t-1}, x_t, and t. However, in standard practice, t is usually encoded as a positional or learned embedding and then concatenated into the input rather than treated as a real-valued input. While the domain is written as R^{N} × R^{N} × R, this might mislead a reader into thinking t is a continuous scalar fed directly. In reality, for discrete timesteps, t would be embedded. But since the paper specifies 'time-dependent' and t is discrete (from 1 to T), it's likely that t is handled via embedding — this is standard in diffusion models. The functional form is not strictly wrong, and the implementation details can resolve this. So, while slightly imprecise in how t is incorporated, this does not cause a real methodological issue, especially since the paper later refers to time embedding in Sec. 5. This is a minor clarity issue.

comments.title: Underspecification of adversarial training procedure across t
comments.explanation: Equation (5) appears to suggest that the discriminator Dφ is trained jointly across all t ≥ 1, but the notation Dφ(x_{t-1}, x_t, t) suggests a single shared discriminator with t as input. Is Dφ shared across timesteps or is there a separate discriminator per t? The paper does not clarify. In conditional GANs with timesteps, this is a critical design choice: sharing enables parameter efficiency; separate discriminators may improve step-specific fidelity. The generator training objective also lacks specification of whether θ includes generator parameters per t or a shared generator conditioned on t. The text mentions a 'time-dependent discriminator' but doesn't say whether it's shared or not. This lack of specification makes reproduction difficult. For example, StyleGAN uses global discrimination, but diffusion models often condition on t. While time conditioning via embedding (as mentioned later) resolves some of this, the extent of sharing is not stated. Given that the model's success may hinge on this architectural choice, this omission is significant.

comments.title: Missing Equation After 'Similarly, we define _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) by:'
comments.quote: Similarly, we define _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) by:
comments.explanation: The text states 'Similarly, we define _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) by:' and immediately continues with a description of the GAN generator, but no equation is present. In the context of the surrounding formalism—especially the earlier definition of the DDPM denoising model as _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) := _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t,_ **x** 0 = _fθ_ ( **x** _t, t_ ))—it is expected that a parallel formal definition would follow. The absence of a displayed equation here is disruptive to the logical flow and risks misleading a reader about whether a formula was simply omitted by OCR or if the definition is meant to be purely implicit. While the subsequent prose describes how **x** _0 is generated via _Gθ_ ( **x** _t, **z**, t) and then **x** _t−1 is sampled from _q_ ( **x** _t−1 | **x** _t, **x** _0), the lack of a consolidated expression such as _pθ_ ( **x** _t−1 | **x** _t) = ∫ _q_ ( **x** _t−1 | **x** _t, **x** _0) pθ( **x** _0 | **x** _t) d**x** _0 or equivalent weakens clarity and reproducibility. This is a real gap: even if the meaning is recoverable from context, a formal model definition belongs here. The OCR log shows this line was likely corrupted as it ends abruptly with 'by:' and then jumps into prose without a formula.

comments.title: Ambiguous Use of _pθ_ ( **x** _0 | **x** _t ) Without Definition
comments.quote: where _pθ_ ( **x** 0 _|_ **x** _t_ ) is the implicit distribution imposed by the GAN generator _Gθ_ ( **x** _t,_ **z** _, t_ )
comments.explanation: The notation _pθ_ ( **x** _0 | **x** _t_ ) is introduced suddenly without a formal definition. While it is described as 'the implicit distribution imposed by the GAN generator', the exact mapping from _Gθ_ ( **x** _t, **z**, t) to _pθ_ ( **x** _0 | **x** _t_ ) is not specified. In generative modeling, when a generator uses a latent variable **z** ∼ _p_(**z**), the induced distribution is the pushforward measure: _pθ_(**x** _0 | **x** _t) = _p_(**z**) ◦ _Gθ_(⋅ | **x** _t). This should be stated or referenced for clarity. The issue is compounded by the reuse of the subscript _θ_—used earlier for the denoising mean _μ_θ_—now being repurposed for the generator parameters. While parameter sharing via _θ_ is plausible, the lack of specification of how _Gθ_ relates to the denoising distribution _pθ_(**x** _t−1 | **x** _t) creates ambiguity. A careful reader could reasonably ask whether _θ_ refers to the same set of parameters throughout. Given the central role of this implicit distribution in the model's multimodality, its underspecification is problematic.

comments.title: OCR Artifacts in Mathematical Expressions Undermine Readability
comments.explanation: Although this line appears in a different section of the paper, its presence nearby and the fact that similar OCR issues (e.g., corrupted summation or expectation symbols like '') appear in surrounding text raises concern about reliability. In the passage under review, the expression involving D_KL in the footnote: ' _Tt_ =1[E] _[q]_[(] _[t]_[)][ [] _[D]_[KL][ (] _[q]_[(] **[x]** _[t][−]_[1] _[|]_ **[x]** _[t]_[)] _[∥][p][θ]_[(] **[x]** _[t][−]_[1] _[|]_ **[x]** _[t]_[))]]' is clearly corrupted by OCR—summation symbol missing, brackets mismatched, formatting broken. While this is in a footnote, the expression is critical: it defines an alternative baseline (conditional VAE) that failed in early experiments. The corruption makes it difficult to verify what objective was actually tested. This could mislead a reader attempting to reproduce the ablation. The summary defines D_KL correctly elsewhere, so the issue is likely OCR, but the result is still an underspecified method component.

comments.title: Claim of 'Softened Reverse KL' Divergence Lacks Justification
comments.quote: In this case, _D_ adv takes a special instance of f-divergence called _softened reverse KL_ (Shannon et al., 2020), which is different from the forward KL divergence used in the original diffusion model training in Eq. 3
comments.explanation: The paper states that the non-saturating GAN objective corresponds to a 'softened reverse KL' divergence, citing Shannon et al. (2020). While it's standard that GAN objectives minimize f-divergences and that non-saturating loss relates to a form of reverse KL, the term 'softened reverse KL' is nonstandard and not defined in the paper. Moreover, Eq. 3 (referred to here) uses forward KL (D_KL[q||p]) to match the reverse process, whereas GAN training typically does not minimize any KL directly but instead a Jensen-Shannon or other f-divergence. The claim that it's 'reverse KL'—even softened—needs justification. In fact, the non-saturating loss maximizes E[log D(x)] + E[log(1−D(G(z)))], which relates to JS divergence, not reverse KL. Reverse KL would emphasize mode coverage differently. Unless Shannon et al. (2020) precisely defines 'softened reverse KL' as equivalent to the non-saturating loss, this appears to be a mischaracterization. This is not a fatal flaw, but it risks confusing readers about the divergence structure of their training objective vs. standard diffusion models.

comments.title: Missing Justification for Gaussian Form of _q_ ( **x** _t−1 | **x** _0, **x** _t )
comments.quote: The distribution _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** 0 _,_ **x** _t_ ) ... always has a Gaussian form for the diffusion process in Eq. 1, independent of the step size and complexity of the data distribution (see Appendix A for the expression of _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** 0 _,_ **x** _t_ )).
comments.explanation: The claim that _q_ ( **x** _t−1 | **x** _0, **x** _t ) is always Gaussian is correct for the linear Gaussian diffusion process defined in Eq. 1, as it is a conditional distribution of a jointly Gaussian process. However, the assertion is made without any brief derivation or reference to a standard result (e.g., conditional Gaussians under linear systems). While the paper refers to Appendix A, its absence in the provided context leaves the claim unjustified in the main text. For a reader unfamiliar with Kalman filtering or Gaussian conditioning, this might seem like a leap. The claim is correct, but the lack of even a one-sentence justification (e.g., 'since all transitions are linear and Gaussian, the joint distribution is Gaussian, and conditionals of Gaussians are Gaussian') constitutes a skipped non-trivial step. In a rigorous theoretical paper, such a claim would typically be accompanied by minimal justification.

comments.title: Ambiguous interpretation of recall score comparison
comments.quote: we see that GANs have limited sample diversity, as their recall scores are below 0.5. In contrast, our model obtains a significantly better recall score, even higher than several advanced likelihood-based models, and competitive among diffusion models.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this claim appears strong: the authors assert that their model substantially outperforms GANs in sample diversity via a recall score above 0.5 (0.57) versus GANs all below 0.5. However, upon inspecting Table 1, it's evident that several diffusion models achieve recall scores similar or slightly higher (e.g., LSGM at 0.61, Score SDEs at 0.59). The phrase 'significantly better' could mislead a reader into thinking there's a large gap, while the difference between 0.57 and, say, 0.61 is small and may not be statistically significant. Additionally, the threshold of 0.5 is presented as a meaningful boundary, but no justification is given for why 0.5 is a critical cutoff for 'limited diversity.' The comparison is factually correct but framed in a way that may overemphasize the margin of improvement. Context resolves that results are numerically accurate, but the prose risks overstating the practical significance.

comments.title: Latent variable conditioning described vaguely in reproducibility-critical section
comments.quote: We let the latent variable **z** control the normalization layers. In particular, we replace all group normalization layers (Wu & He, 2018) in NCSN++ with adaptive group normalization layers in the generator, similar to Karras et al. (2019); Huang & Belongie (2017), where the shift and scale parameters in group normalization are predicted from **z** using a simple multi-layer fully-connected network.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly motivate the use of adaptive normalization to allow **z** to influence generation, which is crucial for achieving multimodal outputs. However, key implementation details are underspecified. While they reference StyleGAN-style AdaGN, they do not specify the architecture of the 'simple multi-layer fully-connected network'—how many layers, widths, activation functions, or whether it acts globally or per block. Furthermore, it’s unclear if **z** is used only in normalization or also injected elsewhere (e.g., input concatenation). In high-performance GANs, such design choices significantly affect results. The omission could hinder reproducibility, especially since the generator's stochasticity (via **z**) is central to the model’s expressiveness. The reference to prior work helps but doesn't fully resolve the underspecification, as those works use different architectures and settings (e.g., StyleGAN uses learned styles, not raw **z**). This is not a flaw in concept but a gap in method transparency.

comments.title: Inconsistency between denoising step count and sampling time in comparison
comments.quote: our sampling time is about 2000 × faster than the predictor-corrector sampling by Song et al. (2021c) and ∼20 × faster than FastDDPM (Kong & Ping, 2021).
comments.explanation: This comparison draws a strong claim about speedup. From Table 1, our model uses 4 NFE and 0.21s, while Score SDE (PC) uses 2000 NFE and 423.2s. The ratio of 423.2 / 0.21 ≈ 2015, which aligns with '2000× faster'—so the claim is numerically sound on clock time. FastDDPM with 50 steps takes 4.01s, so 4.01 / 0.21 ≈ 19.1, justifying '∼20×'. However, the comparison is potentially misleading because the speedup depends heavily on the baseline’s step count. For instance, DDIM with T=50 achieves 4.01s, but DDPM with T=1000 takes 80.5s. The authors are technically correct in citing the specific baselines, but the lack of qualification (e.g., 'compared to full-step baselines') might lead readers to assume *all* prior diffusion models are this slow. In context, the point stands—our model is dramatically faster for competitive quality—but the magnitude of '2000×' hinges on comparing against the most expensive sampling scheme. Since the claim is accurate as stated and the figure supports it, this is a mild framing issue rather than an error.

comments.title: Unjustified equivalence claim between T=1 and unconditional GAN
comments.quote: Note that T = 1 corresponds to training an unconditional GAN, as the conditioning **x** _t_ contains almost no information about **x** 0.
comments.explanation: The intuition here is that with only one denoising step from a highly perturbed **x** _1_, the input **x** _1_ ≈ noise, so the generator effectively ignores it and behaves like an unconditional GAN. This is plausible and consistent with the ablation results showing degraded recall. However, the claim that it 'corresponds to' an unconditional GAN is an overstatement. Even if **x** _t_ is noisy, the generator is still formally conditioned on it—it might still exploit residual correlations. More importantly, in a true unconditional GAN, there is no conditioning variable at all, while here the architecture still processes **x** _t_ as input, even if unused. The model is *functionally similar* but not equivalent. A careful reader might be misled into thinking the architecture reduces exactly to an unconditional GAN, which it does not. The ablation helps justify the practical similarity, but the conceptual conflation remains. This is a minor logical overreach rather than a technical flaw.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Direct Denoising' and 'Noise Generation' Parametrizations
comments.quote: **Parametrization for** _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) **:** We study two alternative ways to parametrize the denoising distribution for the same _T_ = 4 setting. Instead of letting the generator produce estimated samples of **x** 0, we set the generator to directly output denoised samples **x** _t−_ 1 without posterior sampling ( _direct denoising_ ), or output the noise _ϵt_ that perturbs a clean image to produce **x** _t_ ( _noise generation_ )...
comments.explanation:
At first glance, the description of 'noise generation' seems inconsistent with the diffusion formulation. The text says the generator outputs the noise _ϵt_ that 'perturbs a clean image to produce **x**_t_', which would mean **x**_t_ = √ᾱ_t **x**_0 + √(1−ᾱ_t) _ϵt_ in standard DDPM notation. However, in this model, the generator is used in the reverse process, so it should output something related to denoising, not forward diffusion.

I checked the context: the authors contrast this with their main approach, where **x**_0 is generated via **z**, then **x**_{t−1} is sampled using the posterior. In 'noise generation', they suggest predicting _ϵt_, presumably as part of estimating **x**_0 (since **x**_0 can be recovered from **x**_t and _ϵt_), and then proceeding to compute **x**_{t−1}. This would align with the usual diffusion practice (e.g., Ho et al., 2020), where _ϵ_ is predicted to recover **x**_0.

The phrasing 'that perturbs a clean image to produce **x**_t' is misleading—it describes the forward process, not the reverse modeling goal. A careful reader might think the generator is being used to simulate the forward pass, which would be incorrect. The authors likely mean that _ϵt_ is defined as the noise that *would have* been used in the forward process to reach **x**_t from **x**_0, and estimating it is a way to indirectly estimate **x**_0.

While experts in diffusion models will likely interpret this correctly, the wording risks confusion, especially for readers less familiar with the bidirectional role of _ϵ_. The description should emphasize that _ϵt_ is predicted from **x**_t to recover **x**_0 (or the denoising direction), not to simulate forward diffusion.

comments.quote:

**0.57**

comments.explanation:
Initially, I noticed that in Table 2, the FID score for T=4 is 3.75, which is quite low for CIFAR-10 and competitive with advanced GANs. Later, in the text under 'High Resolution Images', the model achieves FID 7.64 on CelebA-HQ-256 and 5.25 on LSUN Church—both reasonable and in line with expectations. However, the concern was whether the T=4 FID of 3.75 on CIFAR-10 was consistent with prior results in the literature or within the paper’s own comparisons.

I checked: in Table 4 and 5, DDPM achieves 7.89 on LSUN Church and 7.23 on CelebA-HQ, which aligns with known numbers. On CIFAR-10, DDPM typically achieves FID ~3.17 (Ho et al., 2020), so 3.75 is slightly worse but reasonable for a GAN-based reverse process. The degradation for larger T (4.36 at T=8) is also consistent with their claim that T=4 is optimal.

The No latent variable FID of 20.6 is dramatically worse, which makes sense given the importance of multimodality, and matches the significant drop in IS from 9.63 to 8.37.

After cross-checking with known baselines and internal consistency, no actual numerical inconsistency exists. The values are plausible and internally coherent. The initially surprising FID of 3.75 is well-justified by SOTA results.

comments.title: Underdescribed 'One-shot w/ aug' Baseline
comments.quote:
One-shot w/ aug
8.96
13.2
0.25
comments.explanation:
At first, I was confused about what 'One-shot w/ aug' refers to. The term appears in Table 2 without prior definition in the passage under review. I checked the earlier sections and found that the authors mentioned using forward diffusion as data augmentation might be a baseline. Indeed, in Section 5.1 (not fully quoted) they clarify that this baseline trains a standard GAN with augmented samples from the forward diffusion process.

However, within the current passage, this variant is introduced abruptively. A reader might not realize it’s a GAN baseline that uses noisy samples during training, rather than a one-step version of their model. The label 'One-shot w/ aug' could be misinterpreted as their own T=1 model with augmentation, but from context in the summary, T=1 *is* the one-shot unconditional GAN.

The passage later says: 'Using forward diffusion as data augmentation in a standard GAN training framework yields worse results than the full denoising diffusion GAN', which matches this row. But without that forward context, the row is underspecified. It would help to briefly clarify in the caption or text that this is a traditional GAN trained on noised data via forward diffusion—as a control for whether augmentation alone explains gains.

comments.title: Claimed 2000× Speed-up Not Explicitly Verified in Passage
comments.quote: achieving about 2000 _×_ speed-up in sampling compared to the predictor-corrector sampling by Song et al. (2021c) on CIFAR-10.
comments.explanation:
The abstract claims a 2000× speed-up, and in the stroke-based synthesis section, a 1100× speed-up is quantitatively verified (0.16s vs 181s). However, in the main CIFAR-10 experiments (Table 2), no NFE or wall-clock time is reported, and the speed-up is not empirically validated within the provided text.

I checked: T=4 implies 4 denoising steps, while Song et al. (2021c) use 1000+ steps in predictor-corrector mode. Even conservatively, 1000 / 4 = 250×, so 2000× seems too high unless their model is *also* faster per step. But the network is NCSN++, similar to Song’s, so per-step cost should be comparable.

The 2000× claim may rely on comparing to a corrector-heavy scheme where each step involves multiple network evaluations (e.g., 2–4 corrector steps), but even then, 1000 × 2 = 2000 evaluations vs 4 gives only 500×. To reach 2000×, either their per-step cost is much lower (unlikely) or the baseline uses many more steps.

While such speed-ups are plausible in spirit, the specific factor is presented as a measured result but not demonstrated in the provided section. The 1100× number for stroke generation is concrete and trustworthy. The 2000× on CIFAR-10 should either be qualified (e.g., 'up to 2000×') or supported by NFE numbers.

comments.title: Missing Justification for Why Larger T Degrades Performance
comments.quote: We observe that... degradation observed for larger _T_, possibly due to capacity limitations.
comments.explanation:
In Table 2, performance degrades from T=4 to T=8 (FID 3.75 → 4.36, Recall 0.57 → 0.56). The authors attribute this to 'capacity limitations', suggesting the model cannot handle more complex conditional modeling across more steps.

This is plausible, but not well-justified. In standard diffusion models, more steps usually help—it's not intuitive why increasing T would hurt unless something breaks in training. The authors assume T ≤ 8 is small, so going from 4 to 8 is doubling the steps, but still small compared to 1000.

Alternative explanations: (i) training becomes more unstable with more steps due to compounding generator error, (ii) the fixed model capacity is now distributed over more steps, weakening each, or (iii) overfitting to diffusion noise at intermediate steps.

The claim may be correct, but 'possibly due to capacity limitations' is speculative without ablation (e.g., increasing width/depth at T=8 to test). Given that this is a central design choice (T=4 optimal), a deeper analysis of this degradation would strengthen the argument.

comments.title: Latent Variable Ablation Shows FID Degradation But IS Drop Is More Than Expected
comments.quote:
No latent variable
8.37
20.6
0.42
comments.explanation:
The ablation 'No latent variable' removes **z**, making the generator deterministic—this converts the model into a one-step denoiser per stage, akin to standard diffusion models.

We expect lower diversity (lower recall), but the FID jumps from 3.75 to 20.6—a massive degradation—while IS drops from 9.63 to 8.37. IS drop is moderate (GANs often range 8–10 on CIFAR-10), but FID from 3.75 to 20.6 is drastic—worse than many GANs.

This suggests the deterministic version not only loses diversity but also generates lower-quality samples. This is surprising because deterministic denoisers (like DDPM) achieve FID ~3.17 on CIFAR-10.

So why does the ablated model perform so poorly? Is it because: (i) the GAN-style training without latent **z** collapses or becomes unstable? (ii) the architecture without **z** and adaptive normalization fails to generate realistic detail?

The paper suggests multimodality helps diversity, but this result implies **z** is also crucial for *fidelity*. The explanation 'removing latent variables converts to unimodal' explains diversity loss but not fidelity collapse. There is a missing mechanistic explanation here—e.g., without **z**, the generator may underuse conditioning or lack variability for stable adversarial training.

While not a fatal flaw, the discussion undersells the dual role of **z**: it's not just for multimodality but for training stability or expressiveness. This should be clarified.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'orders of magnitude faster' without quantitative definition
comments.quote: while being orders of magnitude faster at sampling.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'orders of magnitude faster' is used in the conclusion to summarize the speed improvement of denoising diffusion GANs. While the paper earlier reports a 2000× speed-up on CIFAR-10 (which is indeed over three orders of magnitude), the term 'orders of magnitude' is vague when used without qualification. In scientific writing, 'orders of magnitude' typically implies at least a 10× difference per order, but the phrase can be interpreted loosely. Here, since the specific speed-up (2000×) was already demonstrated in Section 5 (implied by context), this statement is not factually incorrect. However, in the conclusion, it is presented generally without anchoring to the specific dataset or method baseline. A careful reader might infer that this speed-up holds universally across datasets and settings, while the actual experiments were performed on specific benchmarks (CIFAR-10, StackedMNIST, etc.). The context likely resolves the concern—readers familiar with the paper’s body would recall the 2000× claim—but the standalone sentence in the conclusion risks overgeneralization. This is a mild overclaim that could mislead a reader skimming the paper.

comments.title: Missing specification of denoising step count in conclusion
comments.quote: enabling us to take large denoising steps.
comments.explanation: The conclusion emphasizes that large denoising steps are enabled by the multimodal denoising distribution, which is a key technical contribution. However, it does not reiterate the specific number of steps used in experiments (e.g., T = 4, as noted in the PAPER SUMMARY). While this information is provided earlier in the paper and in the experimental section, the omission in the conclusion weakens the impact of the claim. Readers relying solely on the conclusion may not appreciate how few steps are actually used (e.g., as few as two). The omission does not constitute an error, but it underspecifies a critical methodological detail that defines the practical impact. Given that the speed-up is directly tied to the reduction in T, restating the concrete step count would strengthen clarity. Since the full context resolves this, and the value of T is clearly stated elsewhere, this is not a major flaw, but it is a missed opportunity for reinforcement.

comments.title: Overstatement of 'overcomes the generative learning trilemma to a large extent'
comments.quote: Our denoising diffusion GAN overcomes the generative learning trilemma to a large extent, allowing diffusion models to be applied to real-world problems with low computational cost.
comments.explanation: This claim, while aligned with the paper’s goals, presents a strong conclusion that may overreach given the evidence. The trilemma involves balancing sample quality, mode coverage, and fast sampling. The paper provides strong evidence for high sample quality (via FID, visual results), mode coverage (via KL on StackedMNIST, mode counts on 25-Gaussians), and speed (e.g., 2000× improvement). However, 'overcoming' the trilemma implies that all three are simultaneously achieved at state-of-the-art levels across diverse data modalities and scales. The experiments are limited to image synthesis on standard benchmarks (CIFAR-10, StackedMNIST, CelebA-HQ, etc.), and no evidence is provided for generalization to other domains (e.g., audio, video, graph generation). Furthermore, while mode coverage is improved over GANs, there is no formal proof or exhaustive test that the method *never* collapses modes or fails on harder distributions. The phrase 'to a large extent' tempers the claim, but 'overcomes' remains a strong verb in academic discourse. A more precise phrasing would be 'makes significant progress toward resolving' the trilemma. Given the careful evidence in the paper, this is not a major flaw, but it is a rhetorical overstatement that could mislead outside the immediate context.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper is exceptionally well-written, characterized by a clear, logical flow and a compelling framing of a significant technical challenge in generative modeling. By identifying the "generative learning trilemma"—the inherent trade-off between sample quality, mode coverage, and sampling speed—the authors provide a strong, intuitive motivation for their work that is immediately accessible to the academic community. The core contribution (replacing the Gaussian assumption in diffusion denoising with a conditional GAN) is both elegant and technically sound, directly addressing the bottleneck of iterative sampling. The writing style is precise and professional, effectively situating the contribution within the existing literature while clearly articulating the research gap. progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper presents a highly motivated and clearly articulated contribution by effectively framing the limitations of current generative frameworks as a "generative learning trilemma," correctly identifying the Gaussian assumption in diffusion reverse processes as a fundamental bottleneck to fast sampling. The proposal to parametrize denoising distributions with conditional GANs to model complex multimodal transitions is theoretically intriguing and promises significant practical value, as evidenced by the ambitious claim of a 2000× speedup on CIFAR-10. However, the manuscript exhibits significant clarity gaps regarding the core mechanism; specifically, it is insufficiently detailed on how adversarial training is stabilized within the diffusion loop to preserve the mode coverage and diversity that diffusion models are known for, raising a critical concern about the risk of reintroducing the mode collapse and training instability that traditionally plague GANs. Furthermore, while the writing is generally lucid, the transition from the Gaussian limitation to the specific GAN-based solution lacks rigorous justification, and the bold assertion of resolving the trilemma must be substantiated by careful ablations demonstrating that the gains in sampling speed do not inadvertently compromise sample fidelity or diversity compared to the baseline diffusion models. The most pressing issue is the apparent tension between leveraging GANs—which are notoriously prone to mode loss—to solve a problem where multi-modality is essential, a challenge that requires immediate clarification in the technical description.

comments.title: Imprecise claim regarding Gaussian marginal and reverse conditional distribution
comments.quote: Second, if data marginal _q_ ( **x** _t_ ) is Gaussian, the denoising distribution _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) is also a Gaussian distribution.
comments.explanation: The authors assert that Gaussianity of the forward marginal $q(\mathbf{x}_t)$ guarantees the reverse conditional $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$ is also Gaussian. Mathematically, this is not generally true. The reverse conditional is given by Bayes' rule: $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t) \propto q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$. Even if the forward transition $q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ is Gaussian, the product with an arbitrary prior $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ will not yield a Gaussian posterior unless $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ itself is Gaussian (or satisfies specific closure properties). In diffusion literature, it is the *conditional* distribution $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0)$ that is explicitly parameterized as Gaussian, not the marginal reverse conditional $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$. The phrasing omits the necessary assumption about the prior $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ or the linear Gaussian structure, which could mislead readers into believing marginal shape alone dictates the reverse process's form. A more precise statement would clarify that under a linear Gaussian forward process with a Gaussian prior $q(\mathbf{x}_0)$, all marginals and conditionals remain Gaussian by construction.

comments.title: Missing formal equation defining the multimodal denoising distribution
comments.quote:
Similarly, we define pθ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) by:

where _pθ_ ( **x** 0 _|_ **x** _t_ ) is the implicit distribution imposed by the GAN generator
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the abrupt transition from 'Similarly, we define pθ(x_{t-1}|x_t) by:' to a descriptive sentence about pθ(x_0|x_t), with no mathematical expression in between. I checked the surrounding text, Figure 3, and standard diffusion literature to resolve this. The authors correctly establish that matching q(x_{t-1}|x_t) requires a complex mixture when steps are large. The missing equation is almost certainly the standard mixture form: pθ(x_{t-1}|x_t) = ∫ pθ(x_0|x_t) q(x_{t-1}|x_t, x_0) dx_0 (or equivalent integral/sum notation). While this is likely an OCR artifact from a displayed equation, its absence breaks the formal link between the GAN generator and the final denoising distribution. Without the explicit formula, the mathematical construction is incomplete for reproducibility purposes.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the generator's sampling procedure during adversarial training
comments.quote: The discriminator is trained by: min_φ ... where fake samples from pθ(x_{t-1}|x_t) are contrasted against real samples from q(x_{t-1}|x_t). Given the discriminator, we train the generator by max_θ ... which updates the generator with the non-saturating GAN objective
comments.explanation: The authors correctly frame the adversarial training using a time-dependent discriminator and the non-saturating objective. However, I initially struggled to see how the generator's gradients would flow given that pθ(x_{t-1}|x_t) is defined via an implicit distribution over x_0. I checked Figure 3 and the 'Advantage over one-shot generator' section, which clarifies that the generator outputs x_0, and x_{t-1} is subsequently sampled from q(x_{t-1}|x_t, x_0). While this two-step sampling process (sample z → generate x̂_0 → sample x̂_{t-1} ∼ q(·|·, x̂_0)) is correct and aligns with reparameterization tricks, the main text does not explicitly state it in the context of the generator objective. A careful reader might not immediately know that the GAN generator must explicitly pass through the stochastic reverse transition q(·|·, x̂_0) to produce the x_{t-1} fed to the discriminator, which is essential for implementing the gradient computation correctly.
